coredata.dc:title: Sustainable red beetroot-derived synthesis of n-doped carbon dots for ratiometric detection of doxorubicin and portable visual sensing platform based on 3D microfluidics paper-based analytical device
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tingting
Guo, Guoqiang
Zhang, Ting
Wang, Linfan
Hu, Houwen
Luo, Xinyu
Wang, Xu
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: It is of great significance to develop fluorescence sensors that are both high-performance and environmentally friendly for on-site monitoring of doxorubicin (DXR), which plays a crucial role in protecting the ecological environment and human health. In this work, a rapid and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensing strategy based on red beetroot-derived carbon dots (N-CDs) is proposed for the determination of DXR. N-CDs are successfully prepared through facile hydrothermal treatment using red beetroot as the sole raw material, which have a cyan emission at 496 nm under the 416 nm excitation. Further, the proposed N-CDs serve as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the ratiometric determination of DXR owing to the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect (FRET). The detection limit (LOD) of DXR is calculated as 78.3 nM within the good linear range of 3-35 μM. Simultaneously, to achieve precise on-site analysis, a real-time fluorescent assay platform is proposed, which combines the portability of 3D microfluidics paper-based analytical device (3D-µPAD) with the analysis capability of smartphone. This platform enables the real-time monitoring of DXR in real samples with acceptable recoveries (98.9 %-103.2 %), presenting the promising application for CDs in on-site detection and drug safety supervision.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red beetroot
Doxorubicin
Ratiometric sensing
Visual discrimination
3D μPAD

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform
Determination of DXR in real samples
Mechanism study of the detection of DXR
Analysis of DXR in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CDs (Inset shows the size distribution of N-CDs). (b) HR-TEM image of N-CDs. (c) AFM image of N-CDs. (d) Raman spectrum of N-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. (f) XPS spectrum of N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (g), N 1 s (h) and O 1 s (i).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. (b) Fluorescent spectra of N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 to 500 nm. (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph map of N-CDs. The fluorescence stability of N-CDs under ultraviolet irradiation for different timescales (d), different storage time in daylight (e) and different concentrations of NaCl (f).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of DXR. (b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F 496/F 596) and the concentration of DXR (Inset: fluorescence images of N-CDs with various concentrations of DXR). (c) Calibration curve of F 496/F 596 and the concentration of DXR. (d) The spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DXR and fluorescent spectra of N-CDs. (e) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs in the absence and presence of DXR. (f) Schematic diagram of the N-CDs sensing DXR.

(a) Schematic diagram of DXR detection using 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform. (b) Photos of 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform with different concentrations of DXR under UV light. (c) Reproducibility of the N-CDs response to DXR with 30 μM and 60 μM. (d) The relationship between G/R and the concentration of DXR.
Schematic diagram of N-CDs for the synthesis process and the application in DXR sensing.
Determinations of DXR in real samples by fluorescence and HPLC.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The critical t-value at the 95% confidence level is 2.78.

head.ce:title: Sustainable red beetroot-derived synthesis of n-doped carbon dots for ratiometric detection of doxorubicin and portable visual sensing platform based on 3D microfluidics paper-based analytical device

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of great significance to develop fluorescence sensors that are both high-performance and environmentally friendly for on-site monitoring of doxorubicin (DXR), which plays a crucial role in protecting the ecological environment and human health. In this work, a rapid and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensing strategy based on red beetroot-derived carbon dots (N-CDs) is proposed for the determination of DXR. N-CDs are successfully prepared through facile hydrothermal treatment using red beetroot as the sole raw material, which have a cyan emission at 496 nm under the 416 nm excitation. Further, the proposed N-CDs serve as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the ratiometric determination of DXR owing to the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect (FRET). The detection limit (LOD) of DXR is calculated as 78.3 nM within the good linear range of 3-35 μM. Simultaneously, to achieve precise on-site analysis, a real-time fluorescent assay platform is proposed, which combines the portability of 3D microfluidics paper-based analytical device (3D-µPAD) with the analysis capability of smartphone. This platform enables the real-time monitoring of DXR in real samples with acceptable recoveries (98.9 %-103.2 %), presenting the promising application for CDs in on-site detection and drug safety supervision.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (DXR), a member of the anthracycline family, has displayed promising antineoplastic properties and been widely applied in cancer therapy, including breast cancer, lung cancer and Hodgkin's lymphoma [1,2]. However, the deleterious effects of DXR are not only specific to cancer cells but also to healthy cells. Therefore, DXR is viewed as a double-edged sword owing to the detrimental effects on various organs [3]. The application of DXR has been severely restricted due to its serious side-effects at high concentration in the blood, such as heart damage, massive alopecia and severe gastrointestinal reaction [4,5]. Its acute cardiotoxicity and dose-dependent form are far more concerning as they can easily induce cardiomyopathy and congestive heart failure, causing damage to patients and cancer survivors [6]. Therefore, it is necessary to prescribe an appropriate amount of drug at a given time and at a lower concentration in order to minimize the toxicity and adverse reactions associated with DXR. At present, analytical approaches for tracing DXR mainly involve high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), Raman spectroscopy and electrochemical methods [7–9]. Although these conventional methods can achieve pinpoint accuracy, they have drawbacks such as expensive instruments, time-consuming procedure and complexity of operation, which impose limitations on their practical application. Hence, there is an immediate need to develop efficient and dependable real-time analytical methods for precise monitoring of the dosage of DXR.

As a novel class of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention due to their attractive properties, including high fluorescence yield, excellent water solubility and low toxicity. These characteristics endow them with the potential for application in optical sensing, biolabeling, photocatalysis and other fields [10–12]. It has been revealed that many fluorescent sensors derived from CDs have been developed due to the superiority of simple operation, cost-effectiveness, high selectivity and sensitivity [13]. For example, Li et al. proposed a fluorescence probe for sensing DXR based on rhodamine 6G-functionalized CDs, achieving the detection limit (LOD) of 0.53 µM [11]. Zhang et al. have prepared nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs through a one-step hydrothermal method, which exhibited high selectivity in determining DXR [14]. However, the majority of CDs are synthesized using harmful chemicals, such as m-phenylenediamine, citric acid, l-histidine and so on, which lead to incalculable contamination during the preparation process [15,16]. Nowadays, the global upsurge in the pursuit of green and sustainable economy has brought attention to the forefront of industry development, including nanotechnology and materials [17,18]. The utilization of various biomass carbon sources, such as tea saponin, avocado seeds, Mopan persimmons, red beetroot and spirulina algae, has been explored for the green synthesis of nanomaterials in recent studies, presenting a promising avenue for research [19–26]. The increasing focus of green synthesis approach is attributed to its cost-effectiveness, sustainability and environmentally friendly nature, contributing to lowering the environment burden. On the other hand, most fluorescence probes based on CDs achieve target detection through changes in the intensity of a single-peak signal, which can lead to inaccuracies and limitations in visual analysis in applications. In comparison, a ratiometric probe utilizes the intensity ratio of two emissions at different wavelengths, building internal self-calibration capability for environmental interference and improving quantification accuracy [27]. Besides, the ratiometric mode also can provide a visual fluorescence response owing to the diverse range of color changes. This makes it more conducive to wide application in portable devices, allowing for intelligent reading of specific targets and field quantitative analysis. Therefore, it is urgent to explore novel CDs fluorescence probes with visual detection capability and develop corresponding portable and integrated devices.

Recently, paper, as a cost-effective and widely available material, has attracted more attention and become a popular tool in chemical and biological analysis [28,29]. Particularly, paper analysis equipment (PAD) shows great promise in real-time detection due to its convenience, low cost and portability, which can not only handle limited small sample volumes, but also be disposed of by incineration [30]. A variety of paper-based sensors have been designed for chemical sensing, such as the BN-CDs based paper sensors developed by Xu et al. for Cu2+/Co2+ sensing [31], the paper-based sensor proposed by Supchocksoonthorn et al. for pyrimethanil sensing and so on [32]. However, most of them are limited to semi-quantitative detection, which visually interprets “yes” or “no” answers from different color changes in the paper by the naked eyes and greatly limits the application in quantitative detection. With the progress and widespread application, smartphones have been widely concerned as an excellent analysis device in the instant detection sensor due to the high-quality image acquisition and efficient data analysis capabilities. The smartphone-assisted paper-based sensors can capture and analyze fluorescence image color values, which is beneficial to accurate and sensitive detection of target analytes.

Based on the abovementioned considerations, a novel kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) produced by a green synthetic method has been developed as fluorometric sensors for DXR, as shown in Scheme 1 . N-CDs are prepared using red beetroot as the only natural raw material through a one-step hydrothermal treatment, exhibiting cyan emission at 496 nm and demonstrating excellent fluorescence stability. Simultaneously, owing to the occurrence of FRET between N-CDs and DXR, the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs decreases gradually while that of DXR increases significantly, enabling the ratiometric determination of DXR. The ratiometric sensing system exhibits an excellent LOD of 78.3 nM and achieves high sensitivity for the quantitative analysis of DXR in real samples. Furthermore, an integrated smartphone-based testing platform is fabricated with the support of a 3D microfluidics paper-based analytical device (3D-µPAD), which is applied in the practical determination of DXR via RGB values analysis and shows a satisfactory LOD of 130.9 nM. Thus, the proposed 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform provides a new approach for accurate, convenient and real-time detection of DXR in medical diagnosis and environmental monitoring fields.

Red beetroot was purchased from the local supermarket. DXR, KCl, NaCl, AgCl, HgCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, CdCl2, PbCl2, FeCl2, AlCl3, l-Cysteine (Cys), alanine (Ala), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), l-Asparagine (Asn), tryptophan (Trp), leucine (Leu), carboxymethyl cellulose (Cac), starch (Sta), sucrose (Sur), glucose (Glu) and additional chemicals were procured from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) without further purification. Deionized water, with a specific resistance of 18.25 MΩ·cm, was generated using a Millipore water purification system and employed consistently during the conducted experiments.

N-CDs were synthesized in a one-step thermal process utilizing red beetroot as the exclusive precursor. In brief, 30 g of red beetroot was crushed and ground using an agate mortar, followed by soaking it in 30 mL of deionized water for 15 min. The resulting solution was then passed through a microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave with a volume capacity of 100 mL. Subsequently, the autoclave was heated to 160 °C for 8 h (Fig. S1, more details about the synthesis optimization of N-CDs are expressed in Supporting information). After cooling down to room temperature, the crude product underwent filtration once again through another microporous membrane with identical specifications (0.22 μm). Further purification was achieved by subjecting the filtrate to dialysis using a bag with the molecular weight cut-off at 3500 Da for an extended duration of 24 h, during which the water within the bag was replaced every six hours. Finally, N-CDs solution was obtained and stored at a temperature of 4 °C until further utilization.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and atomic force microscope (AFM) images were taken using a field emission high-resolution 2100F transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100F, Japan Electron) and an atomic force microscope (Cypher S, Oxford Instruments), respectively. The Raman spectrum was measured with a 532 nm laser using a HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800 spectroscopy. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). Absorption measurements were conducted with a UV-vis absorption spectrometer (UV-2550). Fluorescence spectra (emission and excitation spectra) were determined using the Hitachi F-7000 instrument.

To stabilize the titration of DXR, a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with a pH range of 6.5-7.5 was employed. Initially, various concentrations (ranging from 3 to 400 μM) of DXR solutions were introduced into the N-CDs solution and thoroughly mixed with 500 μL of PBS. The resulting mixture was then diluted to a volume of 3 mL using water and incubated for one minute. The solution was subsequently transferred into a quartz cuvette for fluorescence measurement with excitation at 416 nm. Meanwhile, interfering substances, including metal ions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe2+ and Al3+), amino acids (Cys, Ala, Pro, Ser, Asn, Trp and Leu) and medicinal excipients (Cac, Sta, Sur and Glu), were added to run the selectivity and competition tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform
ce:section.ce:para: The 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform is composed of two modules: an imaging platform based on 3D μPAD and an analysis module based on the smartphone. The analysis was facilitated by a color recognition application commonly used in smartphones, which could convert color signals into numerical data, benefiting quantitative determination of the target. The imaging process relied on 3D-μPAD, which comprised a three-layer flow passage measuring 5.5 cm × 2.3 cm size. The hydrophobic hollow barriers, featuring specific patterns and sizes, were plotted using a commercially obtained black oil-based marker. The hydrophilic reaction zone, sample zones and channels were delimited directly and other areas on each layer were covered with oil-based ink to prevent excess penetration. Samples and N-CDs were added to their respective zones and traveled through small channels to the reaction zone via passive capillary action. After standing for 3 min, relevant images were recorded using a smartphone under the UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DXR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of proposed sensing strategy was assessed using real samples, including tap water, wastewater and Yongjiang River water. The tap water and wastewater were obtained from the campus of Ningbo University. In brief, each sample (100 mL) underwent triple filtration using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove any impurities. Afterwards, the processed samples were introduced into the sample zone on the initial layer of 3D μPAD. The subsequent steps mirrored the procedures outlined for detecting DXR using the fluorescent assay platform based on the 3D μPAD. Each sample was analyzed at least three times at spiked levels. Meanwhile, HPLC served as a reference method, and the t-test was utilized to assess the difference between the two methods.

The morphology and size of N-CDs are characterized by TEM and AFM. Fig. 1 (a) shows the representative TEM image of as-prepared sample. It can be seen that all the materials are dot-shaped and the size distribution shows that the synthesized N-CDs mainly range from 2-8 nm with an average size of 5 nm in the selected area. High-resolution TEM image (HR-TEM) of N-CDs reveals that the spacing between adjacent lattice planes is 0.24 nm, consistent with the graphite (100) diffraction plane (Fig. 1(b)) [33]. The AFM image in Fig. 1(c) shows that the thickness of N-CDs is around 1.3 nm, supporting the spherical morphology of N-CDs. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 1(d) provides further insights into the structure and degree of graphitization of N-CDs. The presence of two peaks at 1386 cm−1 and 1574 cm−1 is attributed to the D and G bands, respectively, where D band represents the structural disorder of the sp2-domain, and the G band represents the existence of the graphite phase. An I D/I G ratio of 0.81 suggests a stronger degree of graphitization of N-CDs. The above characterizations prove the successful synthesis of N-CDs.

To identify the functional groups associated with the surface of prepared N-CDs, the FT-IR spectrum is depicted in Fig. 1(e). A broad absorption peak around 3425 cm−1 is observed, corresponding to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H. The peak at 2853 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching vibration [34]. Peaks at 1680 cm−1, 1429 cm−1 and 1218 cm−1 testify to the presence of CO, CC and CN, respectively [34,35]. Meanwhile, Fig. 1(f) demonstrates three distinct peaks at 285.5 eV, 399.8 eV and 532.1 eV in the full XPS spectrum of N-CDs, indicating the presence of C, N, and O within N-CDs derived from red beetroot. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s in Fig. 1(g) exhibits three distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.2 eV, which are attributed to different carbon bonding configurations such as CC/CC, CO/CN and CO, respectively [36]. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum shows two fitting bands at 399.8 eV and 406.9 eV, corresponding to the presence of CN bonds and nitrate N (Fig. 1(h)) [37]. Similarly, the O 1 s band is deconvoluted into two components representing CO (531.4 eV) and CO (532.6 eV) in Fig. 1(i) [36]. FT-IR and XPS results indicate the existence of N, O and carbonization related functional groups in N-CDs, which align the fact that red beetroot is rich in amino acids and proteins.

The optical characteristics of N-CDs are investigated by analyzing the UV-vis absorption spectrum, as well as the fluorescent excitation and emission spectra. Fig. 2 (a) illustrates a distinct absorption peak at 288 nm in the UV-vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (purple line), which is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC groups [38]. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs vary when they are excited within a range of 360 to 500 nm, with an interval of 20 nm, suggesting that the emission behavior of N-CDs is influenced by the excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the excitation-emission matrix graph map of N-CDs (Fig. 2(c)) displays a distinct fluorescence region (λ ex = 416 nm, λ em = 496 nm), offering a visual representation of the correlation between emission and excitation wavelengths of N-CDs.

The fluorescence stability of N-CDs solution is explored under irradiation at 365 nm light, long storage time and different NaCl concentrations. Obviously, a negligible fluctuation in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs can be observed when they are either irradiated under 365 nm light (Fig. 2(d)) or stored in the air for a long time (Fig. 2(e)), showing good photostability and photobleaching resistance at room temperature. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs remains constant at a series of NaCl concentrations (0-2 mol/L) in Fig. 2(f), indicating excellent fluorescence stability even in high sodium ion environments. These findings indicate that the fluorescence stability of N-CDs remains consistently high even in complex systems, which is beneficial to the sensing applications in challenging environments.

To evaluate the application of N-CDs as fluorescent probes in quantitative sensing, the relative fluorescence intensities of N-CDs are researched with the introduction of various interferents, including metal ions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe2+ and Al3+), amino acids (Cys, Ala, Pro, Ser, Asn, Trp and Leu) and medicinal excipients (Cac, Sta, Sur and Glu). As demonstrated in Fig. S2, with the exception of DXR, other interferents at equivalent concentrations have no significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, revealing that the fluorescence probe based on N-CDs exhibits exceptional selectivity towards DXR and has bright prospect in the application of chemical sensors. Therefore, the response of N-CDs towards DXR is investigated under the optimal excitation wavelength. As presented in Fig. 3 (a), the fluorescence of N-CDs at 496 nm is gradually quenched with DXR concentration increasing, and the emission at 596 nm exhibits an increasing trend, sending out two reverse signals. Based on this, a ratiometric fluorescence probe is constructed using the emission intensity ratio of F 496/F 596 as the dependent variable in the DXR concentration range of 3-400 μM, where F 496 and F 596 represent the intensity of the emission at 496 nm and 596 nm, respectively. In Fig. 3(b), a negative correlation is observed between the intensity ratio (F 496/F 596) and DXR concentration, accompanied by a noticeable color change from cyan to red under UV light (inset of Fig. 3(b)). Additionally, the ratiometric fluorescence response of N-CDs exhibits a linear relationship with DXR concentration within the range of 3-35 μM (Fig. 3(c)), as demonstrated by the equation F 496/F 596 = -0.0606C DXR + 3.1074 (R 2 = 0.9934). Based on the 3σ/k principle (where σ denotes the standard deviation of 5 blank samples, and k represents the slope of the calibration curve), a satisfactory LOD of 78.3 nM is achieved for DXR detection, surpassing numerous previous studies (Table S1).

Considering the complexity of components in real samples, we have conducted an investigation on the anti-interference performance of fluorescence sensors based on N-CDs for practical applications. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the introduction of interfering substances has a negligible impact on the overall reaction system, confirming the exceptional ability of N-CDs to withstand interference during DXR detection. These results indicate that the N-CDs-based sensing method possesses high specificity in recognizing DXR, providing a promising tool for onsite qualitative determination of DXR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study of the detection of DXR
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of N-CDs decreases with the addition of DXR, and the emission at 596 nm is enhanced, laying the foundation for the proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor to monitor the dosage of DXR quantitatively and accurately. In order to understand the ratiometric sensing mechanism, the optical properties of DXR are first explored with different concentrations ranging from 3 to 400 μM. As presented in Fig. S4(a), the optimal emission peak of DXR under 416 nm excitation is centered in 596 nm, consistent with the emergent emission at 596 nm during the sensing process, implying that the arisen emission of mixed solution may be attributed to DXR fluorescence behavior. Nevertheless, a higher fluorescence intensity of emission peak at 596 nm is observed in the presence of N-CDs, which contrasts with the low fluorescence yield of DXR. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of DXR gradually reduces as the concentration contently increases, showing a different trend in the presence of N-CDs (Fig. S4(b)). These results indicate that the enhanced emission at 596 nm is independent of the DXR concentration and can be attributed to the interactions between N-CDs and DXR. Furthermore, the UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CDs are examined both in the absence and presence of DXR. It is observed that there are no noticeable changes in the UV-visible absorption spectrum after the addition of DXR (Fig. S5). This suggests that no chemical interaction occurs between N-CDs and DXR, indicating no formation of new compounds and ruling out a static quenching mechanism.

According to previous reports, the sensing mechanisms in fluorescence methods mainly comprise FRET and IFE [39–42]. FRET is interpreted as a reduction in the emission energy of the donor, where part of fluorescence emission excites the acceptor fluorescence. Under this mechanism, the emission spectrum of donor always exhibits significant overlap with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor. Similarly, IFE involves a reduction in the excitation energy of donor due to the analyte absorption, where the absorption spectrum of analyte coincides with the excitation spectrum of fluorescent molecule. Fig. 3(d) illustrates the UV-vis absorption spectrum of DXR and the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. It is evident that DXR demonstrates a wide absorption range spanning from 400 to 585 nm, with its peak absorption occurring at 490 nm, which aligns precisely with the emission spectrum of N-CDs, indicating the potential presence of a FRET phenomenon [43]. According to the Förster theory of non-radiative energy transfer, three essential conditions are necessary for FRET generation: (1) The acceptor absorption spectrum should effectively overlap with the donor emission spectrum; (2) Appropriate orientation between the dipole of the acceptor and donor is required; (3) The distance between the acceptor and donor must fall within a range of 10-100 Å. To confirm the occurrence of FRET between N-CDs and DXR, it is possible to calculate parameters such as the distance between acceptor and donor (r), Förster radius (R0 ), and energy transfer efficiency (E) using specific equations as follows [44]:
(1)
E = 1 -
τ D
/
τ
DA
= 1 /
1 +
r /
R 0
6
where R0 and r represent the Förster distance and the distance between the acceptor and the donor, respectively. τD and τDA are the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs without and with DXR. R0 can be given by Eq. (2):
(2)
R 0
= 0.2108
κ
2
× φ ×
n R
- 4
× J
λ
1 / 6
where κ2 refers to the orientation factor between the donor and acceptor transition dipole (typically assumed to be 2/3). n is the refractive index of medium, and φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield of donor. J(λ) is the integral of overlap values, which can be achieved by Eq. (3):
(3)
J
λ
=
∫
0
∞
F D
λ
ε A
λ
λ
4
d λ
where εA(λ) is the molar absorptivity of the acceptor at the wavelength λ. Based on Eqs. (1)–(3), the findings indicate that J(λ) is calculated as 1.99 × 1015 M−1 cm−1 nm4, and r is determined to be 31.9 Å, consistent with the FRET theory (10-100 Å). Hence, it can be inferred that an interaction occurs between DXR and N-CDs. Furthermore, the FRET process is also verified by the decreasing fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs after the addition of DXR [45,46]. Fig. 3(e) illustrates the fluorescence decay curves for N-CDs before and after the addition of DXR. The corresponding decrease trend in the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs (from 5.16 ns to 4.93 ns) demonstrates the energy transfer process between N-CDs and DXR, which not only leads to the quenching of N-CDs fluorescence but also causes an increase in DXR fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3(f)).

To achieve on-site determination of DXR conveniently, the 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform is designed with the support of intelligent device to ensure the reliability and accuracy. The whole DXR sensing process using the proposed platform is implemented through two modules: an imaging platform based on 3D μPAD and an analysis module based on the smartphone. Firstly, 3D μPAD is constructed with three layers according to the working principle (Fig. S6(a)). The top layer of 3D μPAD is designed to be divided into two sample zones and one reaction zone, which are connected through specific channels on the two layers below. The second layer serves as the intermediate layer for the flow of N-CDs solution, and the third layer is designed for the flow of the sample solution. A 3D fluidic channel is constructed by overlapping multiple paper layers in vertical space to facilitate the delivery of fluids to the detection zone. The sample and N-CDs are instilled into the fluidic inlets and disperse across adjacent layers, where they come into contact, react and induce fluorescence changes within the mixed reaction zone. Then the smartphone is applied for image processing, including image acquisition, signal conversion and quantification analysis. The photograph of 3D μPAD is captured using the smartphone camera, and the color signals are accurately converted into corresponding numerical values through a smartphone application, namely specific red (R), green (G) and blue (B) values (Fig. S6(b)). As presented in Fig. S6(c), the fabrication of 3D-μPAD sensing platform involves a straightforward process of outlining the channel, air-drying the parclose and securing layers, providing a novel approach for simple and economical on-site testing. The size design of 3D μPAD is presented in Fig. S7.

The performance of the 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform is further validated in Fig. 4 (a). The sample and N-CDs solutions gradually permeate along the designed channel under the capillary action of paper, proving the feasibility of applying 3D μPAD in the on-site sensing field. It is clearly observed that the color of reaction zone shifts from cyan to red with increasing DXR concentrations (Fig. 4(b)). Meanwhile, twice blind repetitions of the measurement on the proposed platform for N-CDs + DXR (30 μM and 60 μM) solutions are carried out with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.76 % and 0.58 % (n = 8 times), respectively, indicating the great reproducibility of this analytical method (Fig. 4(c)). Subsequently, the ratio of G and R values (G/R) is evaluated with application software and decreases with the addition of DXR ranging from 5 μM to 300 μM, as shown in Fig. 4(d). An outstanding linear correlation is observed between the G/R ratio and DXR concentration (R 2 = 0.9868) within the 5-50 μM range. The calibration equation for the curve is G/R = -0.0756C DXR + 5.0578, while the LOD is determined to be 130.9 nM. These results verify that the proposed 3D μPAD-based portable assay platform can be applied for on-site, rapid and visual recognition of DXR with high sensitivity and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DXR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the viability and achievability of utilizing a 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform for detecting DXR, actual samples such as tap water, wastewater and Yongjiang River water are selected. The assessment is carried out using standard addition methods, where DXR at various concentrations (10 μM, 20 μM and 30 μM) are added to real samples. The results are illustrated in Table 1 . After spiking various concentrations of DXR, the recoveries are found to range from 98.9 % to 103.2 %, with the RSD below 7.04 %. These results suggest that the fluorescent assay platform based on 3D μPAD can accurately and reliably detect DXR in real samples. Furthermore, t-test with HPLC as the reference method shows no statistically significant disparities observed in the outcomes at a confidence level of 95 %, suggesting that the platform exhibits satisfactory precision and can be effectively employed for monitoring DXR in authentic samples.

In summary, this work has successfully created a fluorescent sensor that is highly sensitive and eco-friendly, enabling the rapid and effective detection of DXR in actual samples. The stable fluorescence of N-CDs derived from red beetroot is quenched by DXR, and the emission of DXR is enhanced due to the occurrence of FRET. A ratiometric nanoprobe is constructed for sensing DXR, with the LOD as low as 78.3 nM. Meanwhile, a 3D μPAD-based fluorescent assay platform is also successfully developed for onsite analysis of DXR, where a vivid fluorescence color transition from cyan to red is clearly observed. Through examining the RGB values, the sensing platform demonstrates high sensitivity with a LOD of 130.9 nM and enables the real-time quantitative detection of DXR at the testing site. Thus, this strategy not only provides a simple and portable device for monitoring DXR in real time, but also broadens the application of CDs in high-precision rapid determination.

Tingting Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Guoqiang Guo: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ting Zhang: Supervision, Software. Linfan Wang: Visualization. Houwen Hu: Investigation. Xinyu Luo: Investigation. Xu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Da Chen: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110764.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
